chapters.title: Hook
chapters.scriptText:
Somewhere in Washington D.C., in the late summer of 2023, a phone rings — and it belongs to a senior official, someone who works at the intersection of politics and national security, someone whose calls are, by any reasonable assumption, private.
The call is answered, the conversation happens, and when it ends, a recording of that conversation is quietly saved to a server in an operations center somewhere in Chengdu, China. Nobody in Washington knows this is happening — not the official, not their staff, not the FBI, not the NSA, and not the intelligence agencies whose entire reason for existing is to detect exactly this kind of threat. And it will keep happening, silently and continuously, for more than a year.
In September 2024, the Wall Street Journal broke a story that the US national security establishment immediately scrambled to contain. Chinese state hackers had been living inside the networks of America's largest telecommunications companies — AT&T, Verizon, Lumen, and others — for potentially two to three years, accessing call records, reading unencrypted text messages, and in some cases recording actual audio from phone calls. Senator Mark Warner, chairman of the Senate Intelligence Committee, called it — and I am quoting directly — "the worst telecom hack in our nation's history."
But the detail that most coverage buried, or missed entirely, is this: the attackers did not break in through a zero-day exploit, they did not brute-force an encrypted system, and they did not write sophisticated custom malware to defeat America's defenses. They walked through a door the US government had legally required every phone company to build — and leave accessible — for three uninterrupted decades.
This is the story of Salt Typhoon, the hack that turned America's own surveillance system against itself, and the law signed in 1994 that made every part of it possible.
Everything you are about to hear is drawn from published reporting by US government agencies, congressional testimony, verified journalism, and cybersecurity firm disclosures. No events have been invented or embellished. Every attribution reflects the conclusions of the researchers and agencies whose work made this documentary possible. All sources are linked in the description below.

chapters.title: PART ONE — WHO THEY ARE
chapters.scriptText:
WHO THEY ARE

The group has several names, depending on which threat intelligence firm is tracking them. Microsoft calls them Salt Typhoon — the "Typhoon" suffix being used consistently for Chinese state-sponsored actors — while Trend Micro tracks the same group under the name Earth Estries, and others know them as FamousSparrow, GhostEmperor, or UNC2286. The different names reflect different researchers studying different pieces of the same operation at different points in time, gradually assembling a picture of something far larger and more coordinated than any single firm could see alone.
What every researcher agrees on, regardless of what they call the group, is who they work for. Salt Typhoon is assessed with high confidence to be operated by China's Ministry of State Security — the MSS — which is China's foreign intelligence service, its secret police, and the organization responsible for overseas espionage, counterintelligence, and the surveillance of Chinese dissidents living abroad.
Former NSA analyst Terry Dunlap described the group as "a component of China's hundred-year strategy," and that framing matters considerably for understanding what follows, because this is not opportunistic cybercrime and it is not ransomware. It is generational intelligence collection — patient, methodical, and designed to serve strategic objectives that extend decades into the future rather than quarters.
The group has been active since at least 2019, and in its early years it targeted hotels and government agencies across Southeast Asia and Europe in what appear to have been reconnaissance operations, building a comprehensive picture of who travels where, who stays where, and who meets whom under conditions of official discretion. By 2021, based on the forensic evidence now available, it had shifted its primary focus to telecommunications infrastructure in the United States, where it would remain, completely undetected, for years.

chapters.title: PART TWO — THE LAW THAT BUILT THE DOOR
chapters.scriptText:
THE LAW THAT BUILT THE DOOR

To understand how this was possible, you need to go back thirty years to a law most Americans have never heard of, and to a policy debate that security researchers lost badly — a defeat whose consequences the country is still living with today.
It is 1994, and the internet as we know it barely exists. The FBI is worried, because digital telephone technology is advancing faster than their ability to intercept it. The old analog wiretap infrastructure — copper wire, physical taps — is becoming obsolete, and if nothing is done, the agency argues, law enforcement will effectively go dark, unable to listen in on criminals and spies even when they hold a valid court order. Congress accepts this argument, and on October 25, 1994, President Clinton signs the Communications Assistance for Law Enforcement Act — CALEA.
The law's core requirement is straightforward: every telecommunications carrier operating in the United States must build wiretapping capabilities into their networks. Not as an optional feature, not as a best-effort arrangement, but as a hard legal mandate. When law enforcement presents a valid court order, the carrier must be technically capable of providing real-time access to the target's calls and messages. In practice, this means every major phone company in America must maintain what the industry calls a "lawful intercept" system — a dedicated technical infrastructure of portals, servers, and special interfaces through which authorized government agents can monitor communications on demand.
The security research community warned immediately and loudly that this was a catastrophic mistake, not because they opposed law enforcement, but because of a principle that is now, in 2026, simply beyond debate: there is no such thing as a backdoor that only works for the good guys. A backdoor does not check credentials or verify the legitimacy of the person attempting to use it — it simply exists, a structural vulnerability built into the architecture of every major telecommunications network in America, mandated by law and maintained continuously for three decades since Clinton signed the bill. When the government expanded CALEA in 2004 to cover internet service providers as well, the security community repeated those warnings with the same urgency. They were ignored with the same confidence.
Salt Typhoon proved them right.

chapters.title: PART THREE — HOW THEY GOT IN
chapters.scriptText:
HOW THEY GOT IN.
The technical entry point for Salt Typhoon was not sophisticated in the way movies portray hacking. There were no dramatic countdown timers, no Hollywood moments of watching code scroll by while racing against a clock.
What there was, was patience, and the systematic exploitation of vulnerabilities that had existed for years — some of them patched, but never applied.
Salt Typhoon's primary entry vector, documented by Trend Micro, Bleeping Computer, and multiple congressional briefings, was the exploitation of known vulnerabilities in edge devices — the equipment that sits at the boundary of a corporate network and the outside world. Routers, firewalls, VPN gateways. Specifically, they repeatedly targeted Ivanti Connect Secure VPN systems, Fortinet FortiGate appliances, and Cisco routers.
These were not zero-day exploits — meaning vulnerabilities unknown to the vendor — but rather what researchers call N-day exploits: flaws that had been publicly disclosed, flaws for which patches had already been issued and distributed, and flaws that multiple congressional aides confirmed the targeted telecom companies had simply never bothered to apply. In some cases, the vulnerabilities dated back to 2018, meaning that six years of available remediation had been ignored entirely. In one particularly striking documented case, a management system protecting critical network infrastructure was secured with nothing more than a basic numeric password — the kind of credential that every basic security guide has warned against for decades.
Once inside the edge device, Salt Typhoon moved with a discipline that made them extraordinarily difficult to detect. Their core technique is what the industry calls Living off the Land — using the network's own legitimate administrative tools to move through it, rather than importing malware that security software might flag. They issued commands through Cisco's native interfaces. They used Windows Management Instrumentation. They leveraged PowerShell. To a network monitoring tool, their activity looked indistinguishable from a system administrator doing routine maintenance.
They then deployed a rootkit called Demodex — a kernel-level implant that conceals its own presence from the operating system and employs anti-forensic techniques to survive investigation. And they installed a modular backdoor called GhostSpider: a tool that lives entirely in memory, leaves no files on disk, and hides its command-and-control traffic inside normal HTTP headers. Even if someone was looking directly at the network traffic, GhostSpider was designed to look like routine web browsing.
In at least one documented environment, Salt Typhoon maintained undetected access for three years.

chapters.title: PART FOUR — THE MASTER KEY
chapters.scriptText:
Getting into the network was only step one, and what Salt Typhoon did next is the reason US senators reached for words like "breathtaking" and "worst in history."
Inside the networks of AT&T, Verizon, and Lumen Technologies, the group located the CALEA lawful intercept infrastructure — the dedicated portals, the surveillance systems, the backdoors that thirty years of federal law had required these companies to build, maintain, and leave accessible — and they walked through them without obstruction.
From that position, the access they obtained was comprehensive in a way that no ordinary network breach achieves.
They could access call metadata — who called whom, when, for how long, from where. For over a million users, concentrated heavily in the Washington D.C. metropolitan area. Senators, officials, defense contractors, intelligence community personnel, political campaign staff — the entire ecosystem of the American national security apparatus, mapped out in call records.
But call records were only half the picture. The metadata also included geolocation data — precise cell tower triangulation showing exactly where a device was at any given moment. Where a Senator slept. Where a defense contractor met a source. Where an intelligence officer traveled at two in the morning. Location data that granular does not just tell you what someone said. It tells you who they are, what they fear, and what they are trying to hide. In the business of counterintelligence, that is sometimes more valuable than the call itself.
They could access the content of unencrypted calls and text messages. Actual audio. Actual words. In a limited number of cases — fewer than one hundred individuals, according to the FBI — they recorded the actual content of phone calls. According to briefings provided to affected campaigns and confirmed by US investigators with high confidence, the targets included staff of the Kamala Harris 2024 presidential campaign, and phones belonging to Donald Trump and JD Vance.
And then there is the damage that most coverage failed to adequately convey.
By accessing the CALEA systems, Salt Typhoon could see the database of active wiretap requests — the full list of phone numbers currently under court-authorized surveillance by US law enforcement and intelligence agencies. That meant that if any of China's intelligence operatives inside the United States appeared on that list, Beijing would know in time to pull their people out, destroy evidence, and redirect operations before a single arrest could be made. The FBI could be closing in on a Chinese spy, building a counterintelligence case for months, preparing to act — and the target would already be gone. The entire US counterintelligence apparatus, built over decades at enormous cost, had effectively been handed to the adversary it was designed to protect against.
One former White House national security official described this to the Wall Street Journal as "a counterintelligence failure of the highest order," and that assessment is precise rather than hyperbolic. Salt Typhoon did not simply steal information — they compromised the machinery that protects information and turned the surveillance system against the people who built it.

chapters.title: PART FIVE — THE SCALE
chapters.scriptText:
By the time the breach became public in late 2024, US officials confirmed nine major American carriers had been compromised. The Big Three — AT&T, Verizon, and Lumen Technologies — plus six regional and infrastructure providers including Charter Communications, Windstream, Consolidated Communications, Spectrum, and Viasat. Between them, these nine carriers handle the communications of the overwhelming majority of Americans.
But this was not simply an American story. By August 2025, the FBI confirmed that Salt Typhoon had hacked at least 200 companies across 80 countries, and the geographic scope — assembled from reporting by TechCrunch, Trend Micro, and multiple government advisories — covers virtually the entire western world. Canada confirmed breaches at its top telecommunications firms. Australia's ASIO director-general publicly confirmed attempts to access critical infrastructure. The UK confirmed a cluster of activity across its telecommunications sector. Singapore confirmed that all four of its major telecoms had been hit, triggering an eleven-month cleanup operation. Across the Five Eyes intelligence alliance and well beyond — South America, Southeast Asia, the Indo-Pacific, Europe — the pattern was the same at every entry point: Cisco routers at the network edge, and behind them the CALEA-style lawful intercept systems that virtually every western government had legally required its telecoms to maintain.
And it did not stop with telecommunications. In December 2025, intrusions were detected in several United States House of Representatives committees and subsequently attributed to Salt Typhoon — meaning that by the end of 2025, the group had moved from wiretapping America's phones to sitting inside America's legislative branch.
Because CALEA is not uniquely American — the UK's Investigatory Powers Act imposes the same capabilities, as do similar laws across much of the developed world — Salt Typhoon did not find one backdoor. They found an entire internationally mandated category of backdoor and worked their way through it systematically, country by country, carrier by carrier.
The Electronic Frontier Foundation, which had opposed CALEA since the day it passed in 1994, published a single sentence in response to the breach that captured three decades of ignored warnings: "There is no backdoor that only lets in good guys and keeps out bad guys."

chapters.title: PART SIX — THE COVER-UP THAT WASN'T
chapters.scriptText:
In December 2024, AT&T issued a statement. "We detect no activity by nation-state actors in our networks at this time."
Read that sentence again. Carefully.
They did not say no data was stolen. They did not say the breach had not occurred. They did not say anyone's communications were safe. They said they did not currently detect activity.
There is something else that barely made headlines. When the breach was first discovered, US officials attempted to keep the names of the nine compromised carriers out of the press entirely. The FBI and the intelligence community wanted to manage the disclosure — contain the story, control the narrative, avoid the market and public reaction that would follow. That effort failed. The Wall Street Journal obtained the names and published them. The story broke not because the government chose transparency, but because it couldn't stop a leak.
Verizon's statement was similarly precise: "Verizon has contained the cyber incident." An "independent and highly respected cybersecurity firm" had confirmed containment. They declined to name the firm.
Senator Maria Cantwell, ranking member of the Senate Commerce Committee, was not satisfied. In June 2025, she wrote to the CEOs of AT&T and Verizon demanding documentation — the actual remediation plans, the vulnerability assessments, the records of what had been accessed and when. Both companies confirmed the existence of reports by Mandiant, a forensics firm, that would contain exactly this information. Neither company provided the reports.
Eight months later, in February 2026, Cantwell wrote again — this time to a Senate committee chairman, demanding that the telecom CEOs testify in person. Her letter cited expert testimony that the networks were still not fully secure. That Salt Typhoon may still have active footholds. That the scale of the network — tens of thousands of endpoints — made it effectively impossible to guarantee complete eradication.
There was one more institutional failure that received almost no coverage.
In January 2025, the US Treasury Department sanctioned Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology Co., a Chinese cybersecurity company it accused of having direct involvement with Salt Typhoon — making it the only named entity the US government officially linked to the operation. The sanction was real, but its practical effect was limited: the company operates in China, beyond the reach of American courts, and the operatives behind it continue their work regardless.
In March 2025, the Trump administration fired every member of the Cyber Safety Review Board before it could complete its investigation of the Salt Typhoon breach. The board had been specifically convened to produce a definitive public account of what happened, how it happened, and what structural changes needed to follow — and that report will now never be written, because the investigation was terminated and the accountability process was quietly closed before it could produce findings that might have been inconvenient for anyone.

chapters.title: PART EIGHT — THE DIMENSION NOBODY IS DISCUSSING
chapters.scriptText:
There is a dimension to Salt Typhoon that rarely appears in the coverage, and that requires a moment to sit with properly.
The US intelligence community has assessed, in public documents, that China's goal in the event of a military conflict — specifically a conflict over Taiwan — would be to disrupt American command, control, and communications infrastructure in order to slow US military mobilization and create confusion in the critical early hours. Salt Typhoon's target selection was not random. The concentration in Washington D.C., the focus on government officials, defense contractors, military networks, and intelligence community personnel, the systematic targeting of the backbone infrastructure through which US military and intelligence communications flow — all of it aligns with that strategic objective in ways that are too consistent to be coincidental.
A former NSA analyst described Salt Typhoon as a component of China's hundred-year strategy, and that framing is the correct lens for understanding the full operation. The calls intercepted in 2023 and 2024 are not simply today's intelligence. They are the foundation for a comprehensive understanding of how the US government communicates under pressure, who speaks to whom, what the command structures look like when decisions need to be made quickly, and which relationships and channels carry the most weight. That picture does not expire. It is stored, analyzed, and built into models that will inform decisions years and potentially decades from now.
Between March and December 2024, Salt Typhoon also compromised an unnamed US state's Army National Guard network for nine undetected months, exfiltrating administrator credentials, network traffic diagrams, geographic location maps, and personally identifiable information on service members. Given that National Guard units in fourteen states are integrated with state fusion centers responsible for sharing threat information, that single compromise potentially undermined cybersecurity coordination across an entire layer of US infrastructure that most Americans never think about until it fails.
Salt Typhoon obtained, in the words of Deputy National Security Advisor Anne Neuberger, "broad and full access" to American telecommunications — the capability to geolocate millions of individuals and record calls at will. That is not a data breach in the conventional sense. That is strategic pre-positioning for a conflict that has not yet begun.

chapters.title: PART SEVEN — WHAT THE TELECOMS KNEW
chapters.scriptText:
The post-mortem on Salt Typhoon revealed a pattern that is, by now, familiar from every major breach in the history of corporate cybersecurity: the vulnerabilities were known, the patches existed, and the companies had simply not applied them.
Congressional aides confirmed that some of the exploited vulnerabilities dated back to 2018, meaning that patches had been issued and security advisories had been published years before the breach, and the telecoms had received the same notifications as every other organization in their sector — they simply chose not to prioritize remediation against the cost and operational disruption of doing so.
And then there is the detail that should make every American who pays a phone bill feel something between rage and despair. One of the most critical management systems — the kind that sits at the heart of a major carrier's network infrastructure and that Salt Typhoon needed to access in order to move laterally through the entire organization — was protected by nothing more than a basic numeric password. Not a complex credential, not multi-factor authentication, but a string of numbers of the kind that security awareness training has warned against for as long as security awareness training has existed. This is not a billion-dollar company being defeated by nation-state tradecraft so sophisticated that no private organization could counter it. This is a billion-dollar company leaving a master key under the doormat and being surprised when someone picked it up.
Senior national security officials, speaking after the breach, blamed what they called "rudimentary cybersecurity failures" — not sophisticated exploitation, not zero-day attacks, but the basic hygiene, patch management, and password policy that every security framework has recommended for decades and that companies in this sector had the resources, the expertise, and the regulatory obligation to implement.
The response to this, from the regulatory side, has been actively counterproductive. In its final weeks in office, the Biden administration's FCC pushed through new rules requiring telecommunications carriers to implement stronger security controls specifically around lawful intercept systems — rules written in direct response to what Salt Typhoon had demonstrated was possible. Within months of taking office, the Trump administration's FCC revoked those rules entirely, leaving the same architectural vulnerabilities legally unaddressed that had allowed the breach to persist undetected for years.
As of early 2026, CALEA remains law, the backdoors that enabled the breach are still legally required, and the telecoms must still maintain the lawful intercept systems whose architecture Salt Typhoon exploited across nine carriers in America and dozens of networks worldwide. The FBI responded to all of this by announcing a ten-million-dollar bounty for information on Salt Typhoon operatives — and as of the time this script was written, nobody has collected it.

scenes.script: In its final weeks in office, the Biden administration's FCC pushed through new rules requiring telecommunications carriers to implement stronger security controls specifically around lawful intercept systems — rules written in direct response to what Salt Typhoon had demonstrated was possible.
scenes.reasoning: Highlights the timing of the regulatory change.

scenes.script: Within months of taking office, the Trump administration's FCC revoked those rules entirely, leaving the same architectural vulnerabilities legally unaddressed that had allowed the breach to persist undetected for years.
scenes.reasoning: Shows the reversal of security progress.

scenes.script: As of early 2026, CALEA remains law, the backdoors that enabled the breach are still legally required, and the telecoms must still maintain the lawful intercept systems whose architecture Salt Typhoon exploited across nine carriers in America and dozens of networks worldwide.
scenes.reasoning: Shows the global scale of the vulnerability.

scenes.script: The FBI responded to all of this by announcing a ten-million-dollar bounty for information on Salt Typhoon operatives —

chapters.title: PART NINE — THE LESSON
chapters.scriptText:
In the days after the breach became public, the FBI did something that captured the entire situation in a single institutional moment: its cybersecurity officials publicly urged Americans to stop using regular phone calls and text messages for anything sensitive, recommending instead that people switch to Signal, FaceTime, and end-to-end encrypted applications. The agency whose predecessor law — CALEA — had created the vulnerability was now telling the public to route around that vulnerability, because the system it had spent three decades insisting was secure had just been used by a foreign intelligence service to read its own wiretap logs.
The debate over encryption backdoors has been running since 1994. Law enforcement has consistently argued that end-to-end encryption — the kind that cannot be intercepted even by the carrier — makes it effectively impossible to investigate criminals and terrorists, and so they have pushed for keys, for backdoors, for lawful access mechanisms that preserve the government's ability to read what it needs to read. The security community has consistently responded that no such system can be built securely, that any mechanism designed to allow authorized access will inevitably be exploited by unauthorized access, and that a backdoor cannot distinguish between a federal judge issuing a lawful order and a foreign intelligence service looking for an open door.
Salt Typhoon is not a theoretical argument in that debate. It is the empirical proof. The system built for authorized access became the entry point for the most consequential telecommunications breach in American history, China read American wiretaps for years without detection, and the FBI is now urging people to use tools that make CALEA-style interception technically impossible. The argument is settled. The wrong side won it in 1994, and the country has spent the years since building the infrastructure that would eventually make that defeat catastrophic.
There is no clean resolution to this story. Salt Typhoon remains active — as of early 2026, despite sanctions, despite the ten-million-dollar bounty, and despite public attribution by every major Western intelligence agency, the group continues to breach telecommunications networks around the world. The FCC has acknowledged that vulnerabilities are still being exploited. Security researchers have warned that some of the compromises may never be fully remediated, that networks of the scale involved make complete eradication effectively impossible to verify, and that in some cases the attackers may simply have built access paths too deeply embedded to find. CALEA remains law, and the next group willing to use it against America is already looking for a way in.

chapters.title: EPILOGUE — THE PHONE CALL
chapters.scriptText:
Somewhere in Washington D.C., in the late summer of 2023, a phone rings — and the person who answers it has no reason to think anything is wrong. Their network is provided by a company that employs thousands of cybersecurity professionals. Their government runs agencies whose entire budget is dedicated to detecting exactly this kind of threat. Their calls are protected, they believe, by the most advanced surveillance infrastructure in the world.
They answer, and in Chengdu, a recording begins.
It is still there.
